issues.description: Agent was unaware of existing TUI (`gc tui`) when creating manual test plan. Created CLI-only test scenarios, missing a major feature. Only discovered when user asked about TUI testing.
issues.impact: Test plan was incomplete. Could have made architectural decisions without considering TUI implications. Demonstrates gap in codebase awareness during planning tasks.
issues.suggestion: Before creating test plans or making design decisions, agent should explicitly scan for all registered commands (check main.go) and major subsystems (check internal/ directories). Consider adding a 'codebase inventory' step to context bundle building.

issues.description: Self-learning command (`gc self-learn`) is designed in docs/command-design.md but not implemented. Issue collection infrastructure exists but no way to analyze or act on collected issues.
issues.impact: Learning loop is incomplete - data is captured but never reviewed. System cannot improve from its mistakes automatically.
issues.suggestion: Implement gc self-learn as Phase 7 priority. Should analyze learning-log.json, identify patterns, propose improvements.

issues.description: TUI has planned features not yet implemented: multi-select, orchestration progress view, brain dump processing, task creation from TUI.
issues.impact: TUI is functional for viewing/state-changes but limited. Users may expect more interactive capabilities.
issues.suggestion: Phase 6 work - prioritize based on user feedback during testing.

issues.description: Test plan included 'gc process --list' command that doesn't exist. Agent wrote test scenarios based on assumed functionality without verifying the actual CLI flags.
issues.impact: Test plan was incorrect, causing confusion during testing. User had to discover the error themselves.
issues.suggestion: Before writing test plans, agent should run '--help' on each command to verify actual flags and options. Never assume functionality exists - verify first.

issues.description: Agent dismissed deployment brain dump as 'not testable' without thinking through verification. User pointed out that 'gc --version' from home directory would verify successful deployment.
issues.impact: Agent provided incomplete analysis of task testability. Could lead to incorrectly categorizing tasks as untestable when they have clear verification paths.
issues.suggestion: When evaluating if a task is testable, explicitly think through: 'What would prove this task is complete?' Even infrastructure/deployment tasks usually have a verification command or state check.

issues.description: Agent claimed 'TUI Feature Parity Sprint Complete' after implementing view-only features (brain dump, task creation, sprint view, orchestration VIEW). However, the core workflow - actually STARTING orchestration from TUI - was missing. User had to point out the gap by asking 'how would I use TUI to accomplish this task?'
issues.impact: User trusted the 'feature parity' claim and could have discovered the gap at a critical moment (e.g., on a deadline) causing significant disruption. False confidence in system capabilities.
issues.suggestion: Before claiming feature parity or completion of a 'parity' sprint: 1) List ALL commands/features from the reference system (CLI), 2) Map each to its TUI equivalent, 3) Verify each mapping includes the FULL workflow not just viewing. Ask: 'Can a user complete the entire workflow without leaving TUI?' For orchestration, viewing progress ≠ starting orchestration.

issues.description: Conflated 'orchestration progress VIEW' with 'orchestration capability'. The brainstorm document said 'Orchestration progress view - Real-time view of pipeline execution' which was implemented correctly, but this is not the same as being able to START orchestration.
issues.impact: The task was technically completed as specified, but the sprint goal ('feature parity') was not achieved. Task completion ≠ goal completion.
issues.suggestion: When working on 'parity' or 'equivalence' goals, the acceptance criteria should be 'can do everything X can do' not 'implemented the listed tasks'. Validate against the goal, not just the task list.

issues.description: Tasks created programmatically (via jq/JSON manipulation) cannot be orchestrated because they lack context bundles. The `gc orc` command requires a context bundle built by `gc create`. When attempting to run `gc orc 12`, sanity check failed with 'task has no context bundle'.
issues.impact: Unable to orchestrate external project (NotifAI) tasks that were created from a designer's task document. The workflow assumed tasks could be created from external specs and immediately orchestrated, but the pipeline has a hidden dependency on context bundle files.
issues.suggestion: Either: 1) Add a `gc prepare` or `gc bundle` command to build context bundles for existing tasks, 2) Make context bundles optional in sanity check with a --skip-sanity flag, 3) Allow the pipeline to build context bundles as its first step, or 4) Document that all tasks MUST be created via `gc create` to be orchestrable.

issues.description: The workflow for using Ground Control on external projects is not documented. Questions unanswered: How do you import tasks from another project's task document? How do you set up context bundles for a different codebase? How does the coder stage know which project to work in?
issues.impact: Blocked from testing Ground Control's ability to orchestrate work on external projects (a key use case). The system appears designed only for tasks within the ground-control repository itself.
issues.suggestion: Document the full workflow for: 1) Creating tasks from external specs, 2) Building context bundles for external projects, 3) Configuring pipeline stages to work in different directories. Consider adding a --project flag to commands.

issues.description: User described a conditional flow: 'sanity check fails, send tasks to context engineer.' Agent interpreted this as: 'Add Context Bundle stage BEFORE Sanity stage.' This is wrong - the correct flow is: 1) Sanity runs first, 2) If sanity fails specifically for missing context bundle, THEN route to Context Engineer, 3) After CE builds bundle, retry Sanity. Agent proposed the wrong architecture and started implementing before user caught the error.
issues.impact: Would have implemented inefficient architecture where Context Bundle stage runs on EVERY task, even those that already have bundles. The correct conditional routing only invokes Context Engineer when actually needed. User had to interrupt and correct.
issues.suggestion: When user describes conditional/branching logic, explicitly restate the flow back to them BEFORE implementing. Use a diagram or numbered steps: '1. X runs, 2. IF condition THEN Y, 3. ELSE Z'. Pay attention to words like 'if', 'when', 'fails' that indicate conditional behavior, not sequential stages. The phrase 'sanity check fails, send to CE' clearly means sanity runs FIRST, not after.

issues.description: Agent started delegating implementation to builder subagent before confirming the architecture was correct. User had to interrupt the Task tool execution.
issues.impact: Wasted a subagent invocation. If the builder had completed, would have needed to redo the work.
issues.suggestion: For architectural changes, confirm the design with user BEFORE delegating to builder. Say: 'Here is the flow I will implement: [steps]. Correct?' Only after confirmation, proceed with implementation.
